title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the --kubeconfig kubelet.conf file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the kubelet.conf file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The kubelet.conf file is the kubeconfig file for the node, and controls various parameters that set the behavior and identity of the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Automated AAC auditing has been modified to allow CIS-CAT to input a variable for the <PATH>/<FILENAME> of the kubelet config file. Please set $kubelet_config=<PATH> based on the file location on your system for example: export kubelet_config=/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf To perform the audit manually: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf Default Value: By default, kubelet.conf file has permissions of 600. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This control requires verification of the file permissions and ownership of the kubelet.conf file on each worker node.
- These file system permissions (e.g., chmod 600, owner root:root) are not exposed via the Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any Kubernetes controller or resource.
- Verification requires direct access to the node’s file system (e.g., via SSH or similar), which is outside the scope of Kubernetes APIs.
- Therefore, this check cannot be automated via Kubernetes-native tooling and must be performed manually.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each worker node in the Kubernetes cluster.
2. Identify the location of the kubelet.conf file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf, but may vary).
3. Run the following command to check the file permissions and ownership:
   ```
   stat -c "%a %U:%G" /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf
   ```
4. Verify that:
   - The permissions are 600 or more restrictive (i.e., only the owner can read/write).
   - The ownership is root:root.
5. If the file permissions or ownership are not compliant, remediate with:
   ```
   chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf
   chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf
   ```
6. Repeat for all worker nodes in the cluster.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.9.0 PDF
